summary: Reflecting on Andrej Karpathy's viral tweet about feeling behind as a programmer, Reis argues that the future of work with AI requires humans to become orchestrators and judges of AI agents. He emphasizes that mastering fundamentals becomes even more critical when working at AI speed, because you need to know what 'good' looks like to effectively evaluate agent output.
key_points: Andrej Karpathy is typically 2-3 years ahead of most people in his observations
The human role is shifting to orchestrator and judge while AI does the work
Mastering fundamentals is essential to move fast with agents
New evaluation methods will be needed as traditional approaches won't scale
This is a once-in-a-lifetime inflection point in the field
money_quotes: Humans sit as the orchestrator and judge, AI goes the work, and we build much faster (to what end is a separate discussion).
If you want to move fast with agents, master the fundamentals! Or don't, and find out what happens…
To be an orchestra conductor or judge, you need to know what 'good' looks like.
We're all going through a once-in-a-lifetime inflection in our field. You can choose to sit on the sidelines, or be a part of the change and build what's next.
key_insight: As AI agents take over execution, the value of human expertise shifts to knowing what 'good' looks like—making mastery of fundamentals more important than ever, not less.
